source.title: Generation of an E. coli platform strain for improved sucrose utilization using adaptive laboratory evolution

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: E. coli K-12 strains engineered with csc genes grow slowly on sucrose, limiting industrial use. ALE was applied to improve growth rate and sucrose uptake, with the goal of generating platform strains comparable to E. coli W. The study evolved two K-12 constructs (MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp) and wild-type E. coli W, then identified causal mutations via sequencing and reverse engineering.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Sucrose is an attractive carbon source, but few E. coli strains metabolize it well. Engineered K-12 strains with csc genes were evolved via ALE. Evolved K-12 clones showed 1.72-fold growth increase and 1.40-fold sucrose uptake increase on sugarcane juice. E. coli W clones increased uptake 1.4-fold without significant growth increase. Sequencing revealed frequent mutations in rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE-rph in K-12, and cscR inactivation in W. Reverse engineering confirmed causality. The strains are valuable platform strains.
process_signals: Initial problem: slow growth of engineered K-12 on sucrose
ALE improved growth and uptake
Mutations in specific genes were recurrent
Reverse engineering validated causal mutations
failures_or_limits: E. coli W did not show significant growth rate increase

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Sucrose from sugarcane is a cheap renewable feedstock. E. coli W is the only safe strain that grows robustly on sucrose. Previous engineering attempts in other E. coli strains resulted in slow growth and instability. This study aims to generate additional E. coli strains with efficient sucrose utilization by integrating the csc operon from W into K-12 MG1655 and applying ALE. The approach uses multiple independent populations to identify key causal mutations via parallel evolution.
process_signals: Motivation: need for non-pathogenic, fast-growing sucrose-utilizing E. coli
Prior attempts: slow growth, plasmid instability
Hypothesis: ALE can improve fitness; parallel evolution reveals causal mutations
Design: integrate cscBKA (without repressor cscR) into K-12 chromosome, then evolve
failures_or_limits: Previous engineered strains had 30% lower growth on sucrose vs glucose

section_gists.heading: Methods and materials
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Strains constructions with cscBKA cassette using homologous recombination
section_gists.gist: The csc gene cluster (cscK, cscB, cscA) was amplified from E. coli W and integrated into the E. coli K-12 MG1655 chromosome at the 186 attB site using pOSIP-KO. Two constructs were made: MGcscBKA and a variant MGcscBKAp with a SNP in cscB (Q353H) previously shown to increase sucrose uptake.
process_signals: Starting point: engineered K-12 strains with csc genes
Two variants: one with a potentially beneficial cscB mutation
Integration site: 186 attB

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution of developing improved fitness in start strains
section_gists.gist: Three biological replicates of each strain (MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp, E. coli W) were evolved in M9 minimal medium with 20 g/L sucrose. Serial transfers (1:100) were performed during exponential phase using an automated liquid handler for ~40 days. OD600 was monitored; transfers occurred at OD ~1.3. Evolution continued until fitness plateaued.
process_signals: ALE design: batch culture, serial transfer, constant selection
Automated platform to maintain exponential growth
Termination criteria: small delta growth rate, 1% passage size

section_gists.heading: Genome sequencing and mutation calling of the ALE derived strains
section_gists.gist: Endpoint populations and isolated clones were whole-genome sequenced. DNA was extracted, libraries prepared, and sequenced on Illumina Nextseq (150 bp paired-end). Reads were mapped to reference genomes (K-12 MG1655 and W) using Breseq. Mutations in populations were reported if frequency ≥20%.
process_signals: Sequencing to identify genetic basis of improved fitness
Both populations and clones sequenced
Mutation frequency cutoff: 20%

section_gists.heading: Validation of mutations causality in rpoB, rpoC and pyrE-rph genes
section_gists.gist: Key mutations in rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE-rph were tested for causality by comparing growth of strains harboring these mutations (from this study or from a previous glucose ALE study) on sucrose and glucose. Strains from the glucose ALE were engineered to contain the csc cassette (GLUALE_csc). Fitness increases relative to the starting strain were measured.
process_signals: Hypothesis: mutations in rpoB, rpoC, pyrE-rph cause fitness increase
Reverse engineering: test mutations in clean background
Cross-study comparison: mutations from glucose ALE also tested on sucrose
methods: Growth assays on M9 sucrose and glucose
Strain construction: adding csc cassette to glucose-evolved mutants

section_gists.heading: Extracellular metabolites and physiological properties
section_gists.gist: Evolved clones were characterized in M9 sucrose. Growth, substrate uptake, and byproduct formation were measured. Supernatants were analyzed by HPLC for sugars and organic acids. Uptake/production rates and biomass yield were calculated.
process_signals: Physiological characterization to understand improved phenotype
Measurements: growth rate, sucrose uptake, acetate production, yield

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Evolution of multiple E. coli strains to grow rapidly on sucrose minimal media
section_gists.gist: ALE was performed on three strains: MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp, and E. coli W. All populations showed increased growth rates. K-12 strains had ~1.49-1.75-fold increase; W had ~1.25-fold. Only one fitness jump was observed per population. Experiments were terminated after ~40 days when growth rate plateaued.
process_signals: ALE successful: all strains improved
K-12 strains had larger relative improvement than W
Single fitness jump suggests few major adaptive events
Termination based on plateau
failures_or_limits: E. coli W had small fold increase due to high initial growth rate

section_gists.heading: Physiological characterization of evolved clones
section_gists.gist: One clone per endpoint population was characterized. Evolved K-12 clones showed increased growth rates on M9 sucrose (up to 0.85 h⁻¹) and on sugarcane juice (up to 0.88 h⁻¹). Sucrose uptake rates increased up to 1.4-fold. Acetate production varied. Biomass yields were similar. Some clones showed no improvement or even lower uptake, indicating heterogeneity.
process_signals: Clonal variation: not all clones improved equally
Some clones had lower uptake than starting strain (e.g., MGcscBKA clone 2)
Growth on sugarcane juice generally mirrored M9 sucrose results
Acetate production increased in some clones
metrics: Sucrose uptake rate fold increase: up to 1.4
failures_or_limits: Clone 2 of MGcscBKA had no growth improvement and lower uptake
Acetate production increased, potentially reducing yield

section_gists.heading: Whole genome sequencing and mutation analysis
section_gists.gist: Sequencing revealed 9 mutated genes/regions in K-12 and 5 in W. Most clones had 1-3 mutations. Key mutations in K-12: rpoB, rpoC, pyrE-rph intergenic deletion. In W: cscR inactivation. No shared key mutations between K-12 and W. Mutations in rpoB and rpoC were in RNA polymerase subunits; pyrE-rph deletion likely relieves pyrimidine deficiency. cscR mutations likely derepress sucrose utilization genes.
process_signals: Parallel evolution: same genes mutated in independent K-12 lineages
Different evolutionary paths for K-12 and W
Mutations suggest targets: transcription (rpoBC), pyrimidine metabolism (pyrE-rph), sucrose regulation (cscR)
failures_or_limits: Not all mutations characterized; some clones had additional mutations

section_gists.heading: Validation of mutational causality by reverse engineering
section_gists.gist: Mutations in rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE-rph were confirmed to increase growth rate on both sucrose and glucose. RNA polymerase mutations gave larger increases than pyrE-rph deletion. Double mutants (pyrE-rph + rpoB) had additive effects. Mutations from glucose ALE also improved growth on sucrose, indicating general fitness benefits.
process_signals: Causality confirmed: mutations are beneficial
Additive effects: double mutants better than singles
Mutations are not sucrose-specific; they also improve growth on glucose
Cross-study validation: glucose ALE mutations work on sucrose
methods: Comparison of single and double mutants
metrics: Growth rate of double mutant pyrE-rph + rpoB E672K: 0.92 h⁻¹ on sucrose
failures_or_limits: Not all mutations from this study were reverse engineered (e.g., some rpoC mutations were only found in clones with additional mutations)

section_gists.heading: Benchmarking of evolved strains performance on sugarcane juice
section_gists.gist: Evolved clones were tested on sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium. Most showed improved growth and sucrose uptake. The best K-12 clone had 1.72-fold growth increase and 1.40-fold uptake increase. E. coli W clones had 1.4-fold uptake increase but no significant growth increase. Acetate production varied. Biomass yields were similar to M9 sucrose.
process_signals: Real feedstock testing: SCJ contains sucrose, glucose, fructose
Improvements observed on SCJ, but some clones underperformed
E. coli W uptake improved but growth did not, possibly due to other limitations
failures_or_limits: E. coli W growth rate did not increase significantly
Some clones had high acetate production
Only one batch of SCJ tested

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial hypothesis: Chromosomal integration of csc genes enables sucrose utilization but growth is slow; ALE can improve fitness.
Sub-hypothesis: The cscB Q353H variant may provide an initial advantage.

During ALE, mutations in global regulators (rpoBC) and pyrimidine metabolism (pyrE-rph) arise and are selected because they enhance growth on sucrose, likely by improving transcription or nucleotide supply.
These mutations are not sucrose-specific but generally beneficial, as they also improve growth on glucose.
In E. coli W, the main adaptive mechanism is inactivation of the repressor cscR, increasing expression of sucrose utilization genes.
The improved strains can serve as platform strains for industrial bioproduction from sucrose.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did some evolved clones not show improvement (e.g., MGcscBKA clone 2)?
What is the mechanistic link between rpoBC mutations and improved sucrose utilization?
Are there pleiotropic effects of these mutations that could affect other phenotypes?
How stable are the mutations and phenotypes in long-term cultures?
Would the strains perform similarly on other sucrose-based feedstocks (e.g., molasses)?
What is the role of other mutations found in clones (e.g., tdcG, baeS)?

events.summary: Only a few E. coli strains metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic.
Previous engineered E. coli strains for sucrose utilization had slow growth rates and instability.
The csc gene cluster (cscB, cscK, cscA) from E. coli W was integrated into E. coli K-12 MG1655 chromosome at the 186 attB site using pOSIP-KO.
ALE was performed on three strains (MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp, E. coli W) in M9 minimal medium with 20 g/L sucrose, with serial transfers during exponential phase for ~40 days.
Endpoint populations and clones were whole-genome sequenced to identify mutations.
Evolved clones were characterized for growth, substrate uptake, and byproduct formation on M9 sucrose and sugarcane juice.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers the abstract, introduction, and methods sections, providing the problem statement, strain construction design, ALE experimental setup, sequencing approach, and validation strategy.

events.summary: ALE was performed on three E. coli strains to improve growth on sucrose.
Only one fitness jump was observed per population.
Clones from endpoint populations were isolated and characterized for growth rate.
One MGcscBKA clone (#2) showed no growth improvement.
Sucrose-evolved clones also grew faster on glucose, indicating mutations are generally beneficial.
Whole genome sequencing was performed on endpoint clones and populations.
No shared key mutations were found between K-12 and E. coli W experiments.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers ALE results, physiological characterization, and initial sequencing findings.

events.summary: The most predominantly mutated genes in evolved K-12 strains were rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE.
Mutations in rpoB and rpoC have been found repeatedly across different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating parallel evolution.
Mutations in pyrE-rph region likely alleviate pyrimidine starvation on minimal media.
In E. coli W, adaptive mutations predominantly affected cscR and cell wall biosynthesis genes mrdB and mrdA.
Reverse engineering confirmed that mutations in rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE-rph confer fitness advantage on both sucrose and glucose.
Fitness advantage of tested mutations was similar on sucrose and glucose, indicating general benefits.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains mutation analysis and reverse engineering validation, directly supporting the paper's process of identifying and confirming causal mutations.

events.summary: Double mutant strains with mutations in pyrE-rph and rpoB conferred higher fitness than single mutants.
Evolved clones were characterized on sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium to evaluate sucrose utilization.
MGcscBKAp starting strain had lower SUR than MGcscBKA, contrary to expectation from a previous study.
Evolved MGcscBKAp clones had higher uptake rates than MGcscBKA clones, suggesting the cscB mutation impact at faster growth rates.
Populations adapted through different trajectories to reach an optimum metabolic state for fast growth on SCJ.
Key mutations were validated using knock-in strains and can be used as cell engineering parts.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains results from SCJ benchmarking, analysis of double mutants, and discussion of key findings.

events.summary: The authors note that a comparative genomic study to associate the observed difference in gene numbers between E. coli W and K-12 was not apparent.
A potentially fruitful approach to address the observed difference would include growth of evolved and unevolved strains with multiple omics assays.
The ALE derived clones and beneficial mutations represent a promising platform for developing a sucrose-based bioproduction chassis.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk is from the conclusion and references, summarizing the study's outcomes and suggesting future directions. It contains analysis of limitations, a hypothesis for future work, and validation of the ALE approach.

evidence_bank.quote: only a few characterized Escherichia coli strains are able to metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic strains
Most of the previous approaches to engineer E. coli to use sucrose as a carbon source were hampered by slow growth rates and phenotypic instability
the integration site of csc DNA sequence was into E. coli 186 attB site using pOSIP-KO integration module
Bacterial cells were serially passaged during exponential growth phase for approximately 40 days using an automated liquid-handler platform
Endpoint population samples were streaked on agar plates to select single clones for whole genome resequencing
Extracellular metabolites and physiological properties
Three independent populations of each starting strain were evolved in batch and in parallel under the strict selection pressure of continuous exponential growth for approximately 40 days
There was one observed fitness jump (i.e., increase in growth rate) for all the populations across all independent ALE replicates.
The increases in growth rates on sucrose M9 were between 1.17 and 1.57-fold for MGcscBKA clones, 1.25–1.38-fold for MGcscBKAp, and 1.06–1.22-fold for E. coli W
there was one isolated clone that had the same fitness, i.e. growth rate, as the starting strain from MGcscBKA (the ‘#2’ clone)
all clones demonstrated an increased growth rate over the start strains without any significant changes to the final cellular density (gCDW/L)

the vast majority of the sucrose-evolved strains also displayed a fast growth phenotype on glucose is an indication that most mutations acquired during the sucrose ALE experiment were beneficial for growth on both sugars
Whole genome sequencing was used to determine the genetic basis of the improved fitness phenotypes
there were no shared key mutations found between the K-12 and E. coli W experiments

The most predominantly mutated genes or intergenic regions identified across multiple independent replicates in the evolved K-12 strains were related to the RNA polymerase subunits rpoB and rpoC, as well as orotate phosphoribosyltransferase, pyrE.
mutations in rpoB and rpoC genes have previously been found repeatedly across all different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating a very high level of parallel evolution
K-12 strains are known to possess a frame shift in rph which leads to pyrimidine starvation on minimal media and can be alleviated by mutations

Strain-specific adaptive mutations in E. coli W clones were predominantly affecting a metabolic regulation pathway targeting the csc operon and cell wall biosynthesis through rod shape determining proteins.
The comparative analysis of reverse engineered strains showed that mutated genes identified in this ALE study conferred a fitness advantage over the starting strain

the fitness advantage of all tested mutations over the starting strain was observed when grown on M9 with either sucrose or glucose as carbon sources, and the fitness level increases for a given mutated strain were roughly similar when grown on sucrose or glucose
when multiple mutations were present in a strain, such double mutant strains conferred a higher fitness advantage over single mutants with the identical mutations
each endpoint clone was characterized on a raw feedstock, sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium

Analysis of the MGcscBKAp starting strain showed a slightly lower SUR than that of the MGcscBKA starting strain. This finding was in contrast to the observation that such a mutation in cscB leads to a two-fold increase in sucrose uptake rate in a different E. coli strain
the evolved MGcscBKAp clones demonstrated higher uptake rates than the MGcscBKA evolved clones

under the selection pressure applied via ALE, the populations adapted through different trajectories in order to reach an apparent optimum metabolic state enabling a fast growth phenotype on sugarcane juice
These mutations were validated using knock-in strains and can be used as cell engineering parts in additional strains of interest.
a comparative genomic study to associate this particular feature was not apparent
A potentially fruitful approach to address this observed difference would likely include growth of the evolved and unevolved E. coli W and K-12 strains along with multiple omics assays
the ALE derived clones and the beneficial mutations represent a promising platform for developing a sucrose-based bioproduction chassis

The two constructs inserted into the K-12 MG1655 host genome differed by a SNP mutation in the cscB gene, resulting in the residue change Q353H, and were labeled as MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp (see Additional file 2: Text S1 for a detailed description).
There was one co-occurrence of a rpoC mutation, T1045P (ACC→CCC), which was observed in experiments starting from both K-12 strains.
Interestingly, mutations in rpoB and rpoC genes have previously been found repeatedly across all different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating a very high level of parallel evolution [22, 32, 33].
The Escherichia coli K-12 "wild types" W3110 and MG1655 have an rph frameshift mutation that leads to pyrimidine starvation due to low pyrE expression levels.
Whole-genome resequencing of Escherichia coli K-12 MG1655 undergoing short-term laboratory evolution in lactate minimal media reveals flexible selection of adaptive mutations.
Use of adaptive laboratory evolution to discover key mutations enabling rapid growth of Escherichia coli K-12 MG1655 on glucose minimal medium.

Moreover, the developed platform strains are suitable as fermentation chassis with an extended capability to also rapidly utilize glucose to the same level as strains evolved to grow solely on glucose $[22]$ ; (2) this study establishes a transferable sucrose utilization cassette for use in K-12 strains where sucrose utilization is not native; and (3) the identification of key reproducibly-occurring mutations using multiple parallel replicate ALE experiments.
5 Causal mutation analysis for fitness improvement on sucrose and glucose for K-12 strains.
To examine the causality of key mutations identified in this study, growth screens were performed for relevant single and double mutant strains of K-12 MG1655.
K-12 strains are known to possess a frame shift in rph which leads to pyrimidine starvation on minimal media and can be alleviated by mutations [37].
MGcscBKA derivative with a variant SNP in cscB Q353H was abbreviated MGcscB-KAp [16].
The selected clones derived from the evolved populations for the K-12 strains exhibited increased carbon uptake rates for both sucrose and glucose, making them suitable for both carbon sources.
Specifically, a strain with a mutation in the intragenic region between pyrE/rph genes and in rpoB (E672K) had higher fitness than strains with either single mutations.
All rpoC mutations originated from this study, as well as the rpoB S621F with the pyrE-rph deletion mutation (i.

The two similar K-12 MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp strains evolved to relatively similar growth rates, with similar dynamics along the course of the ALE, whereas the fold increase in fitness for E.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Engineered E. coli K-12 strains grow slowly on sucrose, limiting industrial use. ALE was applied to improve growth rate and sucrose uptake, aiming to generate platform strains comparable to E. coli W.

canonical_events.summary: Only a few E. coli strains metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic.
Previous engineered E. coli strains for sucrose utilization had slow growth rates and instability.

The csc gene cluster (cscB, cscK, cscA) from E. coli W was integrated into E. coli K-12 MG1655 chromosome at the 186 attB site using pOSIP-KO, creating two constructs: MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp (with a SNP in cscB Q353H).
ALE was performed on three strains (MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp, E. coli W) in M9 minimal medium with 20 g/L sucrose, with serial transfers during exponential phase for ~40 days.
All populations showed increased growth rates; K-12 strains had ~1.49-1.75-fold increase, W had ~1.25-fold. Only one fitness jump was observed per population.
One MGcscBKA clone (#2) showed no growth improvement.
Sucrose-evolved clones also grew faster on glucose, indicating mutations are generally beneficial.

Whole genome sequencing of endpoint clones and populations revealed 9 mutated genes/regions in K-12 and 5 in W. Key mutations in K-12: rpoB, rpoC, pyrE-rph intergenic deletion. In W: cscR inactivation.
No shared key mutations were found between K-12 and E. coli W experiments.
Mutations in rpoB and rpoC have been found repeatedly across different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating parallel evolution. pyrE-rph mutations likely alleviate pyrimidine starvation.
Reverse engineering confirmed that mutations in rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE-rph confer fitness advantage on both sucrose and glucose. Double mutants (pyrE-rph + rpoB) had additive effects.
key_numbers: Growth rate of double mutant pyrE-rph + rpoB E672K: 0.92 h⁻¹ on sucrose

canonical_events.summary: Fitness advantage of tested mutations was similar on sucrose and glucose, indicating general benefits.

Evolved clones were characterized on sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium. Best K-12 clone had 1.72-fold growth increase and 1.40-fold uptake increase. E. coli W clones had 1.4-fold uptake increase but no significant growth increase.
E. coli W did not show significant growth rate increase on SCJ. Some clones had high acetate production. Only one batch of SCJ tested.

edges.reason: General problem of slow growth on sucrose is followed by specific problem of previous engineered strains.
Problem motivates design of engineered strains with csc genes.
Engineered strains are used in ALE experiment.
ALE leads to observed growth rate increases.
One clone did not improve, showing heterogeneity.
Growth improvement on sucrose also seen on glucose.
Sequencing of evolved strains identifies mutations.
Comparison of mutations between K-12 and W shows no overlap.
Specific mutations are analyzed for parallel evolution and known effects.
Hypothesis of causality leads to reverse engineering validation.
Validation shows mutations are generally beneficial, not sucrose-specific.
Validated mutations explain improved performance on SCJ.

must_cover_points: Problem: slow growth of engineered K-12 on sucrose
Design: chromosomal integration of csc genes
Growth rate improvements and fold changes
Key mutations identified (rpoB, rpoC, pyrE-rph, cscR)
Limitations (E. coli W growth, acetate, batch variability)
